title: practical english usage - Part 202
content:
I suggested trying a different approach.

/ suggested that he try a different approach.

e.g. suggest. Subjunctives are particularly common in American English.

Subjunctives (►  232) and -ing forms are also possible after some verbs, I told Andrew that he ought to be careful.

The secretary said that I mustn't park there, (    The-secretary said me not fo I suggested that he should try a different approach.

t/zɑt-elauses, usually with modal verbs.

However, after these and many other verbs, we  can report instructions, etc with We   do not use infinitive structures after suggest (►  598) or (usually) after say.

3 suggest,         say:   infinitives       not   used I didn't know whether to laugh or cry.

Don't tell me what to do. I've forgotten where to put the keys.

He asked her how to make a white sauce. (‘ How  should I make corresponds to a direct question with should.

The  structure question word + infinitive is common (►   111). It often 2  He  asked       her    how     to   ...

The policeman told me not to park there.

I advise you to think again before you decide.

The landlady has asked us to be quiet after nine o'clock.

I told Andrew to be careful.

with offer).

Object          + infinitive is common with ask, advise, tell and order (but not normally Anna has offered to babysit tonight.

He promised to write. She agreed to wait for me.

advice and suggestions) is often reported with infinitives.

Speech relating to actions (e.g. promises, agreements, orders, offers, requests, 1  He  promised          to   write.

262 indirect      speech:    infinitives not ‘ Are you happy? ‘ Am  I happy? No!' (     .. .4f/Whether I'm happy?...) // “ and whether are not normally used in ‘ echo questions’  (►  304).

5 not   used    in  echo    questions The question is if the man can be trusted.

The question is if... is also possible, but less common.

The question is whether the man can be trusted, (complement) Whether we can stay with my mother is another matter, (subject) is normally preferred.

When  a question-word clause is a subject or complement, whether 4 subject,     complement         and   adverbial       clauses Noun Clauses, Direct and Indirect Speech    Section 24 grammar •  263   indirect speech: advanced points INDIRECT: I said he needn't pretend...

You needn't pretend to be sorry.

She said it must  be pretty late and she really must  go.

It must  be pretty late. I really must  go.

I was afraid that it might be too late.

It might be too late.

:  He said it would be nice if he could see me again.

It would be nice if I could see you again.

and had better (►  77).

after past reporting verbs in indirect speech. This is also true of needn't (►  532) The  modals would, should, could, might, ought and must are usually unchanged 3 modal     verbs     in  indirect      speech He announced that profits were higher than forecast.

She just said she was thirty! I don't believe her for a moment.

The Greeks thought that the sun went round the earth, (     ... that the sun clear that the information comes from the original speaker, not from ourselves.

with what he/she said, if we  are not certain of its truth, or if we  wish to make it We   are more likely to change the original speaker's tenses if we  do not agree INDIRECT: The forecast said it will/would be windy tomorrow.

It will be windy tomorrow.

Are you deaj? I asked how old you are/were.

He proved that the earth goes/went round the sun.

The earth goes round the sun.

them, after a past reporting verb. Both structures are common.

can often choose whether to keep the original speaker's tenses or to change the original speaker's present and future are still present and future - a reporter If somebody talked about a situation that has still not changed - that is to say, if 2  reporting       present      and   future     tenses We were glad to hear you (had) enjoyed your trip to Denmark.

I told you Jack (had) phoned this morning, didn't I?
